title: Environment, Shell State, Startup Files, and Sessions · Operations Defense
summary: Repair a command that works interactively but fails in automation by tracing environment and startup differences instead of adding global hacks. Every item uses a fresh case and misconception-specific distractors. Planned artifact: Record independent decisions and explanations for an environment-diff and startup-resolution notebook. Learners progress through prediction, explicit theory, changed-case practice, causal debugging, and evidence defense.
objectives: Distinguish shell variables, exported environment entries, positional parameters, and child-process inheritance.
Trace aliases, functions, builtins, hashed commands, PATH search, and explicit paths with type and command evidence.
Identify login, interactive, non-interactive, and remote shell startup paths before editing configuration.
Preserve previously learned constraints and attach observable changed-case evidence.

starterFiles.shell:
# linux-basics cumulative operations evidence notebook
# Commands run only in the deterministic stateful terminal simulator.
# Record hypothesis, command, expected signal, observation, conclusion, and recovery.

steps.title: Reconstruct the defensible process
steps.instruction: Order the Shell Variables Environment evidence cycle for this quiz task before implementing it.
steps.why: Correct order keeps Shell Variables Environment assumptions visible before execution and verifies its result against a boundary case afterward.

content.text: check-report works in an interactive shell but fails from a service with PATH=/usr/bin:/bin, LANG=C.UTF-8, and HOME=/var/lib/lab. Trace command resolution and startup files without exporting a secret token. Competency probe: Distinguish shell variables, exported environment entries, positional parameters, and child-process inheritance.

content.title: Ordering constraint
content.text: Order work so Shell Variables Environment has a declared contract before execution and a boundary or counterexample after it.

Use the documented misconception as a counterexample target: Assigning a shell variable automatically exports it to children.
